topics.title: Kyoto Travel
topics.description: Quiet shrines, ryokan, craft, and routes for seeing Kyoto with more care.

topics.title: First-Time Japan
topics.description: Practical routes, food confidence, and gentle planning for a first trip to Japan.

topics.title: Japanese Food
topics.description: Menus, alleys, counters, convenience-store saves, and the meals that shape a trip.

topics.title: Shopping in Japan
topics.description: Useful, packable, and culturally thoughtful things worth bringing home.

topics.title: Slow Travel
topics.description: Forests, islands, walking routes, and quieter places where Japan has room to breathe.

areas.title: Tokyo
areas.description: Neighborhood food, shopping, nightlife, and low-friction first-trip decisions.

neighborhoods.name: Yanaka
neighborhoods.note: Slow streets, small shops, and first-day wandering.

neighborhoods.name: Shibuya
neighborhoods.note: Big-city energy that works best when paired with a quieter pocket.

neighborhoods.name: Asakusa
neighborhoods.note: Useful for craft, tradition, and easy transit links.

areas.title: Kyoto
areas.description: Shrines, ryokan, craft, tea, and routes that reward early starts.

neighborhoods.name: Northern Kyoto
neighborhoods.note: Quieter temples, hills, and slower transit days.

neighborhoods.name: Demachiyanagi
neighborhoods.note: A good edge for cafes, riverside time, and local rhythm.

neighborhoods.name: Higashiyama
neighborhoods.note: Best early or late, with careful pacing.

areas.title: Osaka
areas.description: Food-first planning, markets, alleys, and neighborhoods beyond the obvious photo stop.

neighborhoods.name: Namba
neighborhoods.note: A practical base for first nights and late food.

neighborhoods.name: Nakazakicho
neighborhoods.note: Cafes, small shops, and a slower counterpoint to Dotonbori.

neighborhoods.name: Tenma
neighborhoods.note: Compact food energy that rewards small rounds.

areas.title: Yakushima
areas.description: Ancient forest, rain, coastal villages, and slower island planning.

neighborhoods.name: Forest trails
neighborhoods.note: Choose one major route and leave room for weather.

neighborhoods.name: Coastal villages
neighborhoods.note: Useful for scale, meals, and recovery time.

neighborhoods.name: Short walks
neighborhoods.note: Good backup plans when rain changes the day.

areas.title: Setouchi
areas.description: Island art, ferries, sea air, and slow travel between small ports.

neighborhoods.name: Museum islands
neighborhoods.note: Plan around ferry rhythm, not a crowded checklist.

neighborhoods.name: Harbor towns
neighborhoods.note: Good for ordinary time between art stops.

neighborhoods.name: Sunset routes
neighborhoods.note: Worth protecting when the timetable allows.

itineraries.title: 3 Days in Japan
itineraries.description: A tight route for travelers adding Japan to a larger trip or using Tokyo as a short gateway.

steps.label: Day 1
steps.title: Arrive gently
steps.body: Stay in one Tokyo neighborhood, solve cash, transit, and first meal decisions, then stop early.

steps.label: Day 2
steps.title: Food and texture
steps.body: Pair a market or old street with one planned dinner and a low-pressure shopping stop.

steps.label: Day 3
steps.title: One final loop
steps.body: Use the last morning for a compact route near your departure station or airport line.

itineraries.title: 7 Days in Japan
itineraries.description: A first-timer plan that keeps Tokyo and Kyoto readable without turning every day into transit.

steps.label: Days 1-3
steps.title: Tokyo base
steps.body: Use Tokyo for food confidence, practical setup, and one quiet neighborhood day.

steps.label: Days 4-6
steps.title: Kyoto slower
steps.body: Move early, build around shrines and craft, and avoid overloading temple days.

steps.label: Day 7
steps.title: Return cleanly
steps.body: Keep the final day close to your departure route and finish shopping before luggage becomes a problem.

itineraries.title: 10 Days in Japan
itineraries.description: The classic Tokyo, Kyoto, and Osaka route with more space for neighborhoods and food.

steps.label: Days 1-3
steps.title: Tokyo
steps.body: Start with food, transit confidence, and one older neighborhood before the trip speeds up.

steps.label: Days 4-6
steps.title: Kyoto
steps.body: Anchor mornings with quiet places and afternoons with craft, tea, or riverside time.

steps.label: Days 7-9
steps.title: Osaka
steps.body: Make food the structure and let neighborhoods fill the gaps.

steps.label: Day 10
steps.title: Departure buffer
steps.body: Protect time for packing, gifts, and one simple final meal.

itineraries.title: 14 Days in Japan
itineraries.description: A longer plan that adds forest, islands, or walking routes after the classic first-trip core.

steps.label: Days 1-6
steps.title: First-trip core
steps.body: Use Tokyo and Kyoto for orientation, food, shrines, and practical shopping.

steps.label: Days 7-10
steps.title: Choose one extension
steps.body: Pick Yakushima, Setouchi, Fuji, or Kiso Valley instead of collecting all of them.

steps.label: Days 11-13
steps.title: Recover the rhythm
steps.body: Return through Osaka or Tokyo with space for laundry, food, and lower-pressure wandering.

steps.label: Day 14
steps.title: Leave deliberately
steps.body: Use the final day for calm logistics rather than a new major sight.

planning.title: Japan Travel Checklist
planning.description: A practical pre-trip checklist for first-time Japan travelers, designed to avoid last-minute friction.

blocks.heading: Two weeks before
items: Confirm passport validity and entry requirements from official sources.
Reserve any must-have stays or restaurants.
Choose a communication plan and save offline maps.

blocks.heading: One week before
items: Add transit cards, key addresses, and hotel names to your phone.
Check luggage forwarding needs and pack a small day bag.
Prepare written allergy or medication notes if relevant.

blocks.heading: First day in Japan
items: Withdraw or prepare cash for small shops.
Confirm the airport-to-hotel route before leaving arrivals.
Eat simply and sleep early enough to protect the next day.

planning.title: Budget, Transport, and Connectivity
planning.description: A local-first planning page for money, trains, luggage, and phone access without relying on live external feeds.

blocks.heading: Money
items: Carry a mix of cards and cash because small restaurants and rural stops can still be cash-friendly.
Use price ranges as planning cues, then confirm current fares and fees before travel.
Keep tax-free shopping sealed only when it fits your actual travel plan.

blocks.heading: Transport
items: Build days around one or two anchors instead of maximum transfers.
Use station-area stays when the route has several city changes.
Treat luggage forwarding as a comfort tool on multi-city routes.

blocks.heading: Connectivity
items: Choose eSIM, SIM, or pocket Wi-Fi based on device compatibility and group size.
Save hotel addresses, train routes, and emergency notes offline.
Keep a battery plan for long food, shrine, and walking days.

glossary.definition: A small starter served at many izakaya as part of a seating charge.
A local shopping arcade or street, often useful for everyday food and small shops.
A Japanese-style inn, usually shaped by tatami rooms, baths, meal timing, and house rules.
A hot spring bath with etiquette around washing, towels, tattoos, and quiet behavior.
A visitor shopping process that may require sealed bags and passport handling.
A convenience store, useful for breakfasts, tickets, ATMs, and small travel fixes.
A stored-value transit card or mobile wallet setup used for many trains, buses, and small purchases.
Luggage delivery service that can make multi-city routes easier.
A seasonal multi-course meal often associated with ryokan and refined restaurants.
Temple lodging, usually simple and rule-driven, with a focus on quiet and routine.
